families.name: Respiratory Failure
families.notes: J96.0x–J96.9x: Acute/chronic/acute-on-chronic, hypoxic/hypercapnic

clinical_criteria.criterion: PaO2 < 60 mmHg
clinical_criteria.detail: Arterial blood gas showing severe hypoxemia on room air.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Vol. 7 No. 3, Third Quarter 1990, p.14

clinical_criteria.criterion: SpO2 < 90% on room air
clinical_criteria.detail: Persistent oxygen saturation below 90% requiring supplemental oxygen. COPD baseline may be lower.

clinical_criteria.criterion: PaO2/FiO2 ratio < 300
clinical_criteria.detail: P/F ratio indicating acute respiratory failure or ARDS.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Berlin Definition of ARDS

clinical_criteria.criterion: PaCO2 > 50 mmHg with pH < 7.35
clinical_criteria.detail: Acute respiratory acidosis. Elevation of PaCO2 must be accompanied by acidemia to indicate acute failure.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Historical criteria baseline for Type II failure

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must explicitly document 'acute respiratory failure' or 'acute on chronic respiratory failure'.

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ABG PaO2 < 60 or SpO2 < 90%; P/F Ratio < 300
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify 'hypoxic' type based on low O2 levels

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ABG PaCO2 > 50 with pH < 7.35
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify 'hypercapnic' type based on high CO2 and acidosis

specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Same MCC weight but lacks clinical specificity. Query opportunity if ABG/Vitals indicate specific type.

specificity_ladder.description: Acute on chronic respiratory failure with hypoxia
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Documented chronic baseline (e.g., home O2) WITH acute hypoxic exacerbation.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: ABG shows PaO2 < 60 or SpO2 < 90% but physician documents only 'hypoxia'
cdi_query_templates.query: Clinical indicators reveal a PaO2 of [VALUE] / SpO2 of [VALUE]% requiring [O2_SUPPORT]. You have documented 'hypoxia'. Based on these findings, does the patient's condition represent Acute Respiratory Failure? If so, please document the acuity (acute, chronic, acute-on-chronic) and type (hypoxic, hypercapnic, mixed).

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Patient placed on mechanical ventilation or BiPAP without respiratory failure diagnosis
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient required initiation of [BiPAP/Mechanical Ventilation] on [DATE]. Does this intervention reflect a diagnosis of Acute Respiratory Failure? If so, please add to your assessment and specify type.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Documentation states 'respiratory failure' but lacks acuity or type
cdi_query_templates.query: Your documentation notes 'respiratory failure'. For greatest coding specificity, please clarify the acuity (Acute, Chronic, or Acute-on-Chronic) and the type (Hypoxic, Hypercapnic, Unspecified).

common_pitfalls: Coding 'hypoxia' (R09.02) when 'acute respiratory failure' is supported by evidence but not explicitly documented.
Failing to distinguish chronic hypoxemia in COPD patients vs actual acute exacerbation.
Using J96.0x for post-operative respiratory failure; use J95.821 (Acute) or J95.822 (Acute and chronic) instead.
Sequencing: Acute respiratory failure can be Principal if chiefly responsible for admission, unless chapter-specific rules apply (COVID, Obstetrics).

context_modifiers.with_copd: In COPD patients, PaCO2 > 50 may be baseline. Acute hypercapnic failure requires acute acidemia (pH < 7.35) and change from their specific baseline.
context_modifiers.post_surgical: If respiratory failure occurs post-operatively, query if complication vs expected outcome. Complications use J95.82x series, not J96.x.
context_modifiers.with_covid19: Per Guidelines I.C.1.g.1.a, if due to COVID-19, U07.1 is sequenced first, followed by respiratory failure code.

acute.evidence: Sudden onset, identifiable precipitant, aggressive intervention required (BiPAP, intubation, high-flow O2).

chronic.evidence: Longstanding condition, compensatory metabolic alkalosis on ABG, dependent on home O2, no acute worsening.

acute_on_chronic.evidence: Established chronic respiratory failure with acute deterioration requiring increased O2 support above baseline.

families.name: Sepsis / Severe Sepsis / Septic Shock
families.notes: A40 streptococcal, A41 other sepsis, R65.2x severe sepsis

clinical_criteria.criterion: Temperature > 38.3°C (101°F) or < 36.0°C (96.8°F)
clinical_criteria.detail: Fever or hypothermia as part of the systemic inflammatory response. Hypothermia in sepsis carries a worse prognosis.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Singer et al. JAMA 2016;315(8):801-810 — Sepsis-3 clinical criteria

clinical_criteria.criterion: Serum lactate > 2.0 mmol/L
clinical_criteria.detail: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion. Lactate ≥ 4 mmol/L suggests septic shock even without hypotension. Serial trending is clinically important.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Sepsis-3: Lactate > 2 + vasopressors defines septic shock

clinical_criteria.criterion: Acute increase in SOFA score ≥ 2 from baseline
clinical_criteria.detail: Sequential Organ Failure Assessment: PaO2/FiO2, platelets, bilirubin, MAP/vasopressors, GCS, creatinine. Baseline assumed 0 unless prior organ dysfunction documented.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Sepsis-3 defines sepsis as suspected infection + SOFA ≥ 2

clinical_criteria.criterion: Blood cultures obtained (positive or pending)
clinical_criteria.detail: Blood cultures should be drawn before antibiotics but their positivity is NOT required for sepsis diagnosis. A40/A41 codes require identification of the organism when known.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Section I.C.1.d — Sepsis: organism-specific coding

clinical_criteria.criterion: Vasopressor required to maintain MAP ≥ 65 mmHg despite adequate fluid resuscitation
clinical_criteria.detail: Norepinephrine, vasopressin, epinephrine, or phenylephrine. This criterion distinguishes septic shock (R65.21) from severe sepsis without shock (R65.20).
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Septic shock = vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥ 65 AND lactate > 2 despite fluid resuscitation

clinical_criteria.criterion: Evidence of at least one organ dysfunction (AKI, altered mental status, coagulopathy, hepatic, respiratory, cardiovascular)
clinical_criteria.detail: Severe sepsis (R65.20/R65.21) requires documented organ dysfunction. Each organ dysfunction should also be coded separately.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Section I.C.1.d.1.a — Sepsis with organ dysfunction: code the underlying infection, R65.2x, and each organ dysfunction

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must explicitly document 'sepsis', 'severe sepsis', or 'septic shock'. The coder cannot infer sepsis from positive blood cultures, SIRS criteria, or antibiotic use alone.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q4 2016 p.65: Bacteremia vs sepsis; Q2 2019 p.8: Urosepsis defaults to UTI, not sepsis

documentation_requirements.requirement: When the causative organism is identified, the sepsis code should specify it (A41.01 MSSA, A41.02 MRSA, A41.51 E. coli, etc.) rather than using A41.9 unspecified.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.1.d — Code the specific organism when documented

documentation_requirements.requirement: For severe sepsis, provider must document BOTH sepsis AND associated organ dysfunction. R65.2x is assigned as an additional code. For septic shock, provider must document 'septic shock' — shock from other causes uses different codes.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.1.d.1.a: Severe sepsis requires explicit link between sepsis and organ dysfunction

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Blood culture positive for Methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify organism from culture results

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Blood culture positive for Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify MRSA from culture and susceptibility results

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Blood culture positive for Escherichia coli
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify E. coli from gram-negative sepsis

specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Same MCC tier but less specific — query for organism identification from culture results

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Sepsis + documented organ dysfunction (AKI, respiratory failure, coagulopathy, etc.)
Severe sepsis + hemodynamic compromise requiring vasopressors despite adequate fluid resuscitation + lactate > 2
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'septic shock' when vasopressors required

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Positive blood cultures with IV antibiotics but no sepsis diagnosis documented
cdi_query_templates.query: Blood cultures drawn on [DATE] are positive for [ORGANISM]. The patient is receiving IV antibiotics and meets [CRITERIA — e.g., SOFA ≥ 2]. Does this clinical presentation represent sepsis? If so, please document the diagnosis and the causative organism.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Documentation says 'urosepsis' — ambiguous term
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'urosepsis' on [DATE]. Per Coding Clinic guidance, 'urosepsis' defaults to UTI (N39.0), not sepsis. If the patient has systemic sepsis originating from a urinary source, please document 'sepsis due to [organism] secondary to UTI.'

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Sepsis documented but organ dysfunction present without explicit link
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient has documented sepsis and has developed [organ dysfunction — e.g., AKI, respiratory failure]. Is this organ dysfunction a manifestation of the sepsis (severe sepsis), or is it a separate condition?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Patient on vasopressors with sepsis but 'septic shock' not documented
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient has sepsis and required initiation of [vasopressor] on [DATE] to maintain MAP ≥ 65 mmHg. Lactate is [VALUE] mmol/L. Does this represent septic shock?

common_pitfalls: Coding 'bacteremia' (R78.81) instead of 'sepsis' — bacteremia alone does not equal sepsis without systemic manifestation and provider documentation.
'Urosepsis' defaults to UTI (N39.0) per Coding Clinic; if actual sepsis is present, the provider must explicitly say 'sepsis' or 'sepsis due to UTI.'
R65.20/R65.21 are NEVER sequenced first — they are additional codes after the underlying sepsis code (A40/A41).
Each organ dysfunction in severe sepsis must be coded separately in addition to R65.2x.
Sepsis-3 clinical criteria (SOFA ≥ 2) are clinical diagnostic tools — coding still requires explicit provider documentation of 'sepsis.'

context_modifiers.with_uti: If source is urinary, ensure documentation explicitly states 'sepsis due to [organism] secondary to UTI' — not just 'urosepsis.'
context_modifiers.with_pneumonia: If source is pulmonary, sequence depends on reason for admission. Sepsis from pneumonia: A41.x + J15/J18 + R65.2x if severe.
context_modifiers.post_surgical: Postprocedural sepsis requires explicit documentation linking infection to procedure. Use T81.44- (sepsis following a procedure) as additional code.

families.name: Acute Kidney Injury
families.notes: N17.0–N17.9: Tubular necrosis, cortical necrosis, medullary necrosis

clinical_criteria.criterion: Serum creatinine increase ≥ 0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, OR ≥ 1.5x baseline within 7 days
clinical_criteria.detail: KDIGO Stage 1 minimum. Stage 2: 2.0–2.9x baseline. Stage 3: ≥ 3.0x baseline or creatinine ≥ 4.0 mg/dL or initiation of RRT.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: KDIGO AKI Guideline 2012: Definition and staging of AKI

clinical_criteria.criterion: Urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥ 6 hours
clinical_criteria.detail: Oliguria meeting KDIGO criteria. Stage 2: < 0.5 for ≥ 12h. Stage 3: < 0.3 for ≥ 24h or anuria ≥ 12h.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: KDIGO AKI Guideline 2012: Urine output criteria

clinical_criteria.criterion: Elevated BUN with BUN/Cr ratio indicating prerenal, intrinsic, or postrenal etiology
clinical_criteria.detail: BUN alone is insufficient — creatinine trend is the primary diagnostic criterion. BUN/Cr ratio > 20:1 suggests prerenal; normal ratio suggests intrinsic renal.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: KDIGO supplementary material — differential diagnosis

clinical_criteria.criterion: Initiation of hemodialysis, CRRT, or peritoneal dialysis for acute indication
clinical_criteria.detail: Initiation of RRT automatically qualifies as KDIGO Stage 3 AKI regardless of creatinine level.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: KDIGO Stage 3: Initiation of RRT = Stage 3 by definition

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must explicitly document 'acute kidney injury' or 'acute kidney failure' or 'acute renal failure.' Rising creatinine alone is insufficient for code assignment.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q1 2019 p.12: AKI requires explicit provider documentation

documentation_requirements.requirement: When etiology is known, document the specific type: tubular necrosis (N17.0, MCC), cortical necrosis (N17.1, MCC), medullary necrosis (N17.2, MCC). N17.9 (unspecified, CC) has lower severity weight.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.14: Code to highest specificity for renal conditions

specificity_ladder.description: Acute kidney failure with tubular necrosis
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ATN confirmed by clinical presentation (ischemic or nephrotoxic), muddy brown casts on urinalysis, FeNa > 2%
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify tubular necrosis etiology — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.description: Acute kidney failure with acute cortical necrosis
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Bilateral renal cortical necrosis on imaging, typically in obstetric complications or DIC
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify cortical necrosis — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.description: Acute kidney failure with medullary necrosis
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Papillary necrosis documented, often associated with sickle cell, NSAIDs, or diabetes
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify medullary/papillary necrosis — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.specificity_note: CC tier — used when specific type is documented but not N17.0/N17.1/N17.2 (e.g., contrast nephropathy, hepatorenal syndrome)
CC tier only. Query opportunity: if ATN is clinically present, upgrading to N17.0 changes CC → MCC.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Creatinine rising ≥ 0.3 mg/dL or ≥ 1.5x baseline but no AKI documented
cdi_query_templates.query: Serum creatinine increased from [BASELINE] to [CURRENT] mg/dL over [TIMEFRAME]. Does this represent acute kidney injury? If so, can you specify the etiology (e.g., acute tubular necrosis, prerenal, contrast-induced)?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: AKI documented as unspecified (N17.9) but clinical evidence suggests ATN
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented acute kidney injury. The clinical presentation includes [EVIDENCE — e.g., muddy brown casts, FeNa > 2%, ischemic etiology]. Does this represent acute tubular necrosis? Specifying the type allows for more accurate severity classification.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Patient initiated on dialysis/CRRT acutely without AKI documentation
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient was started on [dialysis/CRRT] on [DATE] for an acute indication. Does this represent acute kidney injury? If so, please document the diagnosis and etiology.

common_pitfalls: Coding 'elevated creatinine' (R79.89) instead of AKI when clinical criteria are clearly met but provider hasn't explicitly documented AKI.
N17.9 (unspecified AKI) is only CC; N17.0 (ATN) is MCC — specifying the type can change the DRG tier. Always query for type when clinical evidence supports ATN.
AKI superimposed on CKD: both should be coded. The AKI is coded in addition to the CKD stage, not instead of it.
Contrast-induced nephropathy: code as N17.8 (other AKI) with T36-T50 adverse effect code. This is still only CC, but proper documentation matters for the clinical record.

context_modifiers.with_ckd: AKI on CKD: code both N17.x AND N18.x. The CKD stage should reflect the baseline, not the acute creatinine.
context_modifiers.with_sepsis: AKI as organ dysfunction in severe sepsis: code R65.2x in addition to the sepsis code and N17.x. Link the AKI to the sepsis.
context_modifiers.with_rhabdomyolysis: Rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI: code M62.82 (rhabdomyolysis) + N17.0 (ATN). CK levels and myoglobinuria support the link.

families.name: Heart Failure
families.notes: I50.x: Systolic, diastolic, combined; acute/chronic/acute-on-chronic

clinical_criteria.criterion: BNP ≥ 100 pg/mL or NT-proBNP ≥ 300 pg/mL
clinical_criteria.detail: Supports diagnosis of heart failure. Values correlate with severity but are not diagnostic in isolation. Age-adjusted thresholds for NT-proBNP: > 50y = > 900; > 75y = > 1800.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA HF Guideline: Biomarker recommendations for diagnosis

clinical_criteria.criterion: Echocardiogram showing reduced EF (HFrEF: EF ≤ 40%) or preserved EF with diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF: EF ≥ 50%)
clinical_criteria.detail: EF determines systolic vs diastolic classification. HFmrEF (EF 41-49%) is a separate category. Diastolic dysfunction requires E/e' ratio, LA volume, or TR velocity.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA: Classification by EF

clinical_criteria.criterion: CXR showing pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, or pleural effusions
clinical_criteria.detail: Supportive but not diagnostic. Pulmonary vascular congestion, cephalization of vessels, and Kerley B lines suggest fluid overload.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA: Imaging recommendations

clinical_criteria.criterion: Clinical signs: JVD, peripheral edema, S3 gallop, crackles/rales, hepatojugular reflux
clinical_criteria.detail: Physical examination findings supporting volume overload. Documentation should include specific findings, not just 'volume overloaded.'
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA: Clinical assessment

clinical_criteria.criterion: IV diuretic administration (furosemide, bumetanide) for acute decompensation
clinical_criteria.detail: IV loop diuretics suggest acute or acute-on-chronic exacerbation. Transition from oral to IV diuretics suggests worsening requiring acute management.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA: Acute decompensated HF management

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must document the TYPE of heart failure: systolic (I50.2x), diastolic (I50.3x), or combined (I50.4x). I50.9 (unspecified) should be queried.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q2 2017 p.9: Documentation of HF type; Q4 2020 p.33

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must document ACUITY: acute (x1 = MCC), chronic (x2 = CC), or acute on chronic (x3 = MCC). This is the critical MCC vs CC distinction.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q1 2017 p.47: Acute vs chronic HF documentation impact

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Reduced EF (≤ 40%) with acute decompensation: new onset OR acute worsening requiring IV diuretics, vasodilators, or inotropes
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'acute' systolic HF — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.description: Acute on chronic systolic heart failure
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Known chronic systolic HF with acute exacerbation
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'acute on chronic' — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.specificity_note: CC tier only. If patient is admitted with decompensation, 'acute on chronic' (I50.23, MCC) is likely more accurate.

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Preserved EF (≥ 50%) with diastolic dysfunction and acute decompensation
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'acute' diastolic HF — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.description: Acute combined systolic and diastolic heart failure
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Both systolic and diastolic dysfunction documented with acute decompensation
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'acute' combined HF — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.specificity_note: CC tier — nonspecific. Query for type (systolic/diastolic) and acuity (acute/chronic).

cdi_query_templates.trigger: BNP elevated, IV diuretics given, but only 'CHF' or 'heart failure' documented
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'CHF.' For coding accuracy, could you specify: (1) Type — systolic, diastolic, or combined? (2) Acuity — is this acute, chronic, or acute on chronic? The patient is receiving IV [diuretic] and BNP is [VALUE] pg/mL.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Chronic HF documented but patient presenting with acute decompensation
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'chronic systolic heart failure.' The patient presented with [acute symptoms — dyspnea, pulmonary edema, weight gain] and required IV diuretics. Does this represent an acute exacerbation of the chronic heart failure (acute on chronic)?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Echo shows reduced EF but documentation says 'diastolic heart failure'
cdi_query_templates.query: The echocardiogram shows EF of [VALUE]% which indicates systolic dysfunction. Your documentation notes 'diastolic heart failure.' Could you clarify the type based on the echo findings?

common_pitfalls: 'CHF' alone is nonspecific — query for systolic/diastolic/combined AND acute/chronic/acute-on-chronic. The combination determines MCC vs CC.
Acute HF codes (I50.21, I50.31, I50.41) are MCC; chronic codes (I50.22, I50.32, I50.42) are only CC. This is the single most impactful distinction for DRG.
'Diastolic dysfunction' on echo does not equal 'diastolic heart failure' — the provider must diagnose heart failure, not just the echo finding.
HFpEF patients may have normal EF — document diastolic dysfunction AND heart failure symptoms to support the code.
Fluid overload/volume overload does not equal heart failure — different code (E87.70) unless HF is the cause.

context_modifiers.with_ckd: HF + CKD: ICD-10 assumes a causal relationship per I.C.9.a.2. Code I13.x (hypertensive heart and CKD) rather than separate HF + CKD if hypertension is also present.
context_modifiers.with_afib: Atrial fibrillation with HF is common. Both should be coded separately. Rate vs rhythm control strategy may be relevant.
context_modifiers.with_copd: HF + COPD: BNP may be elevated from right heart strain in COPD exacerbation. Clarify whether HF is truly decompensated or if the COPD is the primary driver.

acute.evidence: New onset or acute decompensation: IV diuretics, BNP markedly elevated above baseline, acute pulmonary edema, new symptoms requiring hospitalization.

chronic.evidence: Stable, compensated heart failure on oral medications. No acute worsening. Established diagnosis in outpatient records.

acute_on_chronic.evidence: Known chronic HF with acute exacerbation: escalation from oral to IV diuretics, new/worsening symptoms, increasing BNP, weight gain from fluid retention.

families.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
families.notes: I21 initial MI (STEMI/NSTEMI/Type 2), I22 subsequent MI

clinical_criteria.criterion: Rise and/or fall of cardiac troponin (cTn) with at least one value above the 99th percentile upper reference limit
clinical_criteria.detail: High-sensitivity troponin preferred. Delta change pattern (rise/fall) distinguishes acute MI from chronic elevation. Must have clinical context — troponin alone does not define MI.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Fourth Universal Definition of MI (JACC 2018;72:2231-64): Criterion 1

clinical_criteria.criterion: ECG changes: new ST elevation, ST depression, T-wave inversions, or new pathological Q waves
clinical_criteria.detail: STEMI: ST elevation ≥ 1mm in 2+ contiguous leads (≥ 2mm in V1-V3). NSTEMI: ST depression ≥ 0.5mm or T-wave inversions ≥ 1mm in 2+ contiguous leads.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2014 AHA/ACC NSTE-ACS Guideline: ECG criteria

clinical_criteria.criterion: Ischemic symptoms: chest pain/pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, radiation to arm/jaw
clinical_criteria.detail: Symptoms of myocardial ischemia. Atypical presentations (nausea, fatigue, dyspnea only) are common in elderly, women, and diabetic patients.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2014 AHA/ACC NSTE-ACS Guideline: Clinical presentation

clinical_criteria.criterion: Angiographic evidence of coronary thrombus, plaque rupture, or significant stenosis
clinical_criteria.detail: Cardiac catheterization confirms culprit lesion and guides intervention (PCI). Angiographic findings support Type 1 MI vs Type 2.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Fourth Universal Definition: Angiographic criteria

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must document the TYPE of MI: STEMI with artery (I21.0x–I21.2x), NSTEMI (I21.4), Type 2 (I21.A1), or unspecified (I21.9). STEMI requires artery specification.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q4 2017 p.44: Type 2 MI coding; Q1 2021 p.30: STEMI artery documentation

documentation_requirements.requirement: For STEMI, the provider should document the specific coronary artery: LAD (I21.02), RCA (I21.11), LCx (I21.21), left main (I21.01). 'STEMI' alone maps to I21.3 (unspecified site).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.9.e.1: STEMI — code to site

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ST elevation in V1-V4, LAD occlusion on angiography
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify LAD territory from ECG leads or cath findings

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ST elevation in II, III, aVF — inferior territory
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify RCA/inferior territory from ECG leads or cath

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Troponin rise/fall pattern + ischemic symptoms/ECG changes WITHOUT persistent ST elevation
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify NSTEMI vs unspecified MI when troponin and ischemic features are present

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Myocardial oxygen supply/demand mismatch without coronary plaque rupture. Common triggers: tachycardia, hypotension, anemia, respiratory failure.
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Type 2 MI is distinct from 'demand ischemia' — the provider must explicitly state 'myocardial infarction type 2.'
Same MCC tier but nonspecific. Query to distinguish STEMI vs NSTEMI vs Type 2.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Troponin elevated with rise/fall pattern but documentation says 'troponin leak' or 'demand ischemia'
cdi_query_templates.query: Troponin values show [VALUES] with a rise/fall pattern. You documented '[THEIR TERM].' Based on the clinical context, does this represent an acute myocardial infarction? If so, is this a Type 1 (plaque rupture) or Type 2 (supply/demand mismatch) MI?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: STEMI documented without specifying the artery territory
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'STEMI.' ECG shows ST elevation in [LEADS]. Catheterization showed [FINDINGS]. Could you specify the involved coronary artery territory (LAD, RCA, LCx, left main)?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: I22 subsequent MI may be applicable — new MI within 4 weeks of initial
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient had an initial MI on [DATE] and now presents with new troponin elevation and [symptoms]. Does this represent a subsequent (new) MI? I22.x codes are used for new MI events occurring within 4 weeks of an initial I21 MI.

common_pitfalls: 'Troponin leak' is not a diagnosis — it's a lab finding. Provider must document the clinical diagnosis: MI, myocardial injury, or non-cardiac cause of troponin elevation.
Type 2 MI (I21.A1) requires the provider to explicitly state 'type 2 MI' or 'myocardial infarction type 2.' 'Demand ischemia' alone does NOT map to MI.
I22.x (subsequent MI) is only used for a NEW MI within 4 weeks of a previous I21 MI. It is NOT for follow-up visits for a prior MI.
Acute MI codes (I21/I22) have a 4-week window. After 4 weeks, use I25.2 (old MI) for ongoing care.
Non-ischemic myocardial injury (I5A) is a separate code for troponin elevation without MI criteria — introduced in ICD-10-CM FY2024.

context_modifiers.with_cardiac_arrest: If MI causes cardiac arrest: sequence the MI first, cardiac arrest (I46.x) second. If patient dies, MI can be sequenced as principal.
context_modifiers.with_pci: PCI during same encounter for STEMI: the procedural codes are on the PCS side. The MI diagnosis still codes as acute during the initial encounter.
context_modifiers.post_surgical: Perioperative MI: document as Type 2 MI (I21.A1) if supply/demand mismatch, or Type 1 if plaque rupture. Add appropriate complication code.

families.name: Cerebral Infarction
families.notes: I63.x: Thrombotic/embolic/occlusion by artery territory

clinical_criteria.criterion: Brain imaging (CT or MRI) showing acute infarction or excluding hemorrhage
clinical_criteria.detail: Non-contrast CT may be normal in first 6-12 hours. MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is more sensitive for early infarct. CT angiography identifies large vessel occlusion.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 AHA/ASA Acute Ischemic Stroke Guidelines: Imaging recommendations

clinical_criteria.criterion: Acute focal neurological deficit: hemiparesis/plegia, facial droop, aphasia, visual field cut, ataxia, dysarthria
clinical_criteria.detail: NIHSS documents severity. Deficits should correspond to a vascular territory. Document onset time for treatment decisions.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines: Clinical presentation and NIHSS

clinical_criteria.criterion: Vascular imaging identifying occluded or stenotic artery
clinical_criteria.detail: CT angiography or MR angiography showing the culprit vessel. Critical for determining the specific I63 code (precerebral vs cerebral, thrombosis vs embolism).
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 AHA/ASA: Vascular imaging for etiology determination

clinical_criteria.criterion: Administration of IV tPA (within 4.5 hours) or mechanical thrombectomy (within 24 hours for LVO)
clinical_criteria.detail: Acute stroke interventions. Their use is strong supporting evidence for acute cerebral infarction diagnosis.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 AHA/ASA: IV alteplase and mechanical thrombectomy recommendations

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must document 'cerebral infarction' or 'ischemic stroke' or 'CVA (ischemic).' Terms like 'stroke' alone are ambiguous — could be hemorrhagic.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q2 2016 p.25: CVA documentation requirements

documentation_requirements.requirement: Document the MECHANISM: thrombosis (I63.0-I63.3), embolism (I63.1/I63.4), or occlusion/stenosis (I63.2/I63.5). Document the ARTERY when known.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.9.d: Code to highest specificity for cerebrovascular conditions

specificity_ladder.description: Cerebral infarction due to embolism of right middle cerebral artery
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Imaging confirms MCA territory infarct with embolic source (cardiac, aortic arch)
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify mechanism (embolism) and artery (MCA) from imaging and clinical evaluation

specificity_ladder.description: Cerebral infarction due to thrombosis of right middle cerebral artery
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: MCA territory infarct with in-situ thrombosis (large artery atherosclerosis)
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify thrombosis mechanism from vascular imaging

specificity_ladder.description: Cerebral infarction due to cerebral venous thrombosis, nonpyogenic
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Venous sinus thrombosis on CTV/MRV causing venous infarction
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Rare but distinct — different etiology and treatment from arterial strokes
Same MCC tier but least specific. Always query for mechanism and artery territory.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Documentation says 'CVA' or 'stroke' without specifying ischemic vs hemorrhagic
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'CVA' on [DATE]. Imaging shows [FINDINGS]. For coding accuracy, is this an ischemic stroke (cerebral infarction) or hemorrhagic stroke? If ischemic, can you specify the mechanism (thrombosis vs embolism) and the involved artery?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Ischemic stroke documented but mechanism/artery not specified
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'ischemic stroke.' CTA/MRI shows involvement of the [TERRITORY]. The likely mechanism appears to be [thrombosis/embolism based on workup]. Could you document the specific artery territory and mechanism?

common_pitfalls: 'CVA' alone is ambiguous — it maps to I63.9 (cerebral infarction unspecified) but could mean hemorrhagic stroke. Always clarify.
TIA (G45.x) is NOT a cerebral infarction. If imaging shows infarct, the diagnosis should be stroke even if symptoms resolved.
Acute stroke codes (I63.x) are used during the initial encounter. For sequelae (after the acute phase), use I69.3x.
Laterality matters: right vs left vs unspecified affects the specific code. Query for laterality documentation when imaging is available.
Stroke occurring during the postoperative period: clarify whether it's a complication (I97.x) vs independent event.

context_modifiers.with_afib: Atrial fibrillation is the most common embolic source. Stroke with AFib strongly suggests embolic mechanism (I63.4x). Document the association.
context_modifiers.with_tpa: If tPA was given, document this in the context of 'acute cerebral infarction' to support code selection and interventional coding.
context_modifiers.with_hemorrhagic_conversion: If initial ischemic stroke converts to hemorrhagic: the ischemic stroke is still the principal. The hemorrhagic conversion is coded separately.

families.name: Malnutrition
families.notes: E40–E46: Kwashiorkor, marasmus, PCM by severity

clinical_criteria.criterion: Unintentional weight loss: ≥ 5% in 1 month, ≥ 7.5% in 3 months, or ≥ 10% in 6 months
clinical_criteria.detail: Must be unintentional. Compare to documented baseline weight. Severe: ≥ 5% in 1 month or ≥ 10% in 6 months. Moderate: lesser degrees with other findings.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: AND/ASPEN Malnutrition Characteristics 2012: Weight loss thresholds

clinical_criteria.criterion: Inadequate energy intake: < 75% of estimated needs for > 7 days (moderate) or < 50% for > 5 days (severe)
clinical_criteria.detail: Documented by calorie count or dietary intake assessment. Includes both oral and enteral/parenteral intake.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: AND/ASPEN 2012: Energy intake assessment

clinical_criteria.criterion: BMI < 18.5 kg/m² (underweight) — severe malnutrition often BMI < 16
clinical_criteria.detail: Low BMI alone is insufficient without other malnutrition characteristics. Document in context of clinical presentation.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: AND/ASPEN 2012: BMI criteria for malnutrition assessment

clinical_criteria.criterion: Low serum albumin (< 3.5 g/dL) or prealbumin (< 15 mg/dL) — supportive but NOT diagnostic alone
clinical_criteria.detail: Albumin is an acute-phase reactant affected by inflammation, hydration, liver disease. It supports but does not diagnose malnutrition. ASPEN recommends against using albumin as the sole indicator.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: ASPEN Consensus Statement 2015: Albumin is NOT a marker of nutritional status in acute illness

clinical_criteria.criterion: Physical findings: muscle wasting, loss of subcutaneous fat, temporal wasting, functional decline
clinical_criteria.detail: AND/ASPEN recommends physical exam-based assessment: subcutaneous fat loss, muscle wasting, fluid accumulation, reduced grip strength.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: AND/ASPEN 2012: Malnutrition physical examination characteristics

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider OR qualified dietitian must document the SEVERITY of malnutrition: severe (E40–E43 = MCC), moderate (E44.0 = CC), or mild (E44.1 = CC). 'Malnutrition' alone maps to E46 (unspecified, CC).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q3 2017 p.24: Dietitian documentation accepted for malnutrition; Q4 2019 p.86

documentation_requirements.requirement: For severe malnutrition: specify protein-calorie type. E43 (unspecified severe PCM) is MCC. If specific clinical features are present: E40 (kwashiorkor), E41 (marasmus), or E42 (marasmic kwashiorkor).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.4: Malnutrition — code to highest specificity

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: ≥ 2 of: severe weight loss (≥ 5% in 1 mo), severely reduced intake (< 50% for ≥ 5 days), severe muscle/fat loss on exam
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'severe malnutrition' — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Moderate weight loss (1-2% in 1 week, 5% in 1 month), moderately reduced intake, moderate physical findings
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Document 'moderate malnutrition' for specificity

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Mild deficits in intake, weight, or physical findings
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: CC tier — same DRG impact as moderate, but accurate documentation matters
CC tier. Query for severity — upgrading to E43 (severe) changes CC → MCC.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Dietitian documents malnutrition without severity specification
cdi_query_templates.query: The dietitian assessed the patient as malnourished. Based on the clinical findings — weight loss of [VALUE], intake of [PERCENT], and physical findings of [FINDINGS] — would you characterize this as mild, moderate, or severe protein-calorie malnutrition?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Low albumin and BMI < 18.5 but no malnutrition diagnosis
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient's albumin is [VALUE] g/dL, BMI is [VALUE] kg/m², and they have documented [weight loss / poor intake / physical findings]. Has a nutritional assessment been completed? Does this clinical picture represent protein-calorie malnutrition, and if so, what severity?

common_pitfalls: Low albumin alone does NOT support malnutrition — albumin is an inflammatory marker, not a nutritional marker in acute illness (ASPEN 2015 consensus).
Cachexia (R64) is a separate diagnosis from malnutrition — it's a metabolic syndrome associated with underlying illness, not inadequate intake.
'Poor PO intake' or 'malnourished' are not codeable diagnoses. The provider must specify 'malnutrition' with severity.
Dietitian documentation of malnutrition severity IS accepted for coding per Coding Clinic — but the physician must agree with the assessment (no disagreement).
Severe malnutrition (E40-E43) is MCC; moderate/mild/unspecified (E44-E46) is CC — a huge DRG impact difference.

context_modifiers.with_cancer: Cancer-associated malnutrition: code both the neoplasm and the malnutrition. Severe malnutrition is common in advanced cancers and significantly impacts DRG.
context_modifiers.with_surgery: Malnutrition increases surgical complication risk. Preoperative nutritional status should be documented for severity assessment.
context_modifiers.with_dysphagia: Dysphagia causing inadequate intake: code both dysphagia (R13.x) and resulting malnutrition. Document the causal link.

families.name: Pneumonia
families.notes: J12–J18 + J10.0x/J11.0x influenza with pneumonia

clinical_criteria.criterion: Chest imaging showing new pulmonary infiltrate, consolidation, or ground-glass opacity
clinical_criteria.detail: New or progressive infiltrate on CXR or CT in the context of infectious symptoms. CT is more sensitive than CXR for early pneumonia.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 ATS/IDSA CAP Guidelines: Radiographic criteria

clinical_criteria.criterion: Sputum culture, blood culture, or respiratory pathogen panel identifying causative organism
clinical_criteria.detail: Organism identification drives specific code selection (J13 pneumococcus, J14 H. influenzae, J15.x other bacteria, J12.x viral). PCR/antigen tests count.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 ATS/IDSA: Microbiologic testing recommendations

clinical_criteria.criterion: Clinical signs: fever, cough (productive or dry), tachypnea, hypoxia, crackles/rhonchi on auscultation
clinical_criteria.detail: Clinical presentation plus imaging finding. At least two clinical features with a new infiltrate support pneumonia diagnosis.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 ATS/IDSA: Clinical diagnosis criteria

clinical_criteria.criterion: WBC > 12,000 or < 4,000, or procalcitonin ≥ 0.25 ng/mL
clinical_criteria.detail: Leukocytosis or leukopenia supports infection. Procalcitonin helps distinguish bacterial from viral and guide antibiotic duration.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: 2019 ATS/IDSA: Laboratory evaluation

documentation_requirements.requirement: When the causative organism is identified, the provider should document it specifically. Organism-specific pneumonia codes (J13–J16) are more specific than J18.x (unspecified).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.10: Code the specific organism when identified

documentation_requirements.requirement: Document the anatomic type when applicable: lobar (J18.1), bronchopneumonia (J18.0), or interstitial. Document aspiration when pneumonia results from aspiration (J69.0).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q1 2018 p.13: Pneumonia type and organism documentation

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Sputum or blood culture positive for Methicillin-susceptible S. aureus
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify MSSA from culture — organism-specific code is more accurate

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Culture positive for MRSA
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify MRSA from culture and susceptibility

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Pneumococcal antigen positive, or culture positive for S. pneumoniae
COVID-19 positive test + pneumonia on imaging. Sequence U07.1 first, then J12.82.
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: No-exclusion code (pdx = -1) — always survives regardless of principal.
MCC — but specifying the organism is preferred when culture data is available.
Only CC — this is the one pneumonia code that is CC, not MCC. Used for immobility-related pneumonia.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Infiltrate on imaging with antibiotics started but no pneumonia diagnosis
cdi_query_templates.query: Chest imaging on [DATE] shows [FINDINGS — infiltrate/consolidation]. The patient has [symptoms — fever, cough, leukocytosis] and is receiving antibiotics. Does this represent pneumonia? If so, is the causative organism known?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Pneumonia documented as unspecified but culture results available
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'pneumonia.' Respiratory culture results show [ORGANISM]. Could you update the documentation to reflect the specific causative organism?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Aspiration event documented but aspiration pneumonia not explicitly stated
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient had a documented aspiration event on [DATE] and subsequently developed [infiltrate/fever/leukocytosis]. Does this represent aspiration pneumonia (J69.0)?

common_pitfalls: J18.2 (hypostatic pneumonia) is the ONLY pneumonia code that is CC instead of MCC — all others are MCC.
Aspiration pneumonia (J69.0) is a separate code from aspiration pneumonitis (J69.0 is used for both, but clinical distinction matters for treatment).
Ventilator-associated pneumonia (J95.851) is coded separately from community-acquired pneumonia — different DRG pathway.
COVID pneumonia: U07.1 is sequenced FIRST, then J12.82. This is a chapter-specific sequencing rule.
Pneumonia in influenza: use the combination code (J10.0x/J11.0x) rather than separate pneumonia + influenza codes.

context_modifiers.with_sepsis: Pneumonia as source of sepsis: sequence depends on admission reason. If admitted for pneumonia with sepsis developing, pneumonia may be principal. If admitted for sepsis, A41.x is principal.
context_modifiers.with_resp_failure: Pneumonia causing respiratory failure: code both. Sequencing depends on which condition occasions the admission.
context_modifiers.with_covid: COVID-19 pneumonia: U07.1 sequenced first per Guidelines I.C.1.g.1.a, followed by J12.82.

families.name: Pressure Ulcers
families.notes: L89.x: By site and stage (stage 3/4 = MCC)

clinical_criteria.criterion: Stage 3: Full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat. Bone, tendon, or muscle NOT visible/palpable.
clinical_criteria.detail: Granulation tissue, slough, or eschar may be present. Undermining and tunneling may occur. Depth varies by anatomical location.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: NPUAP/EPUAP/PPPIA 2019: Stage 3 Pressure Injury definition

clinical_criteria.criterion: Stage 4: Full-thickness skin and tissue loss with exposed or palpable bone, tendon, fascia, or muscle.
clinical_criteria.detail: Slough and/or eschar may be present on some of the wound bed. Often includes undermining and tunneling. Osteomyelitis risk.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: NPUAP/EPUAP/PPPIA 2019: Stage 4 Pressure Injury definition

clinical_criteria.criterion: Document the specific anatomic site: sacrum, coccyx, buttock, hip, heel, elbow, back, head, ankle, or other site
clinical_criteria.detail: Site determines the 4th/5th character of the L89 code. Laterality (right/left/unspecified) affects code selection for paired sites.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Section I.C.12.a: Pressure ulcer site and stage documentation

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider or wound care nurse must document the STAGE using NPUAP staging: Stage 1, 2, 3, 4, unstageable, or deep tissue. Only Stage 3 (L89.xx3) and Stage 4 (L89.xx4) are MCC.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q4 2017 p.108: Pressure ulcer staging and documentation requirements

documentation_requirements.requirement: Document BOTH the anatomic site and laterality (right/left). 'Pressure ulcer' without site maps to L89.9x (unspecified site).
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Section I.C.12.a.2: Code site and laterality

specificity_ladder.description: Pressure ulcer of sacral region, stage 4
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Full-thickness tissue loss of sacrum with exposed bone/muscle — NPUAP Stage 4
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: If bone/tendon/muscle visible → Stage 4 instead of Stage 3

specificity_ladder.description: Pressure ulcer of sacral region, stage 3
specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Full-thickness skin loss of sacrum with visible subQ fat, but no bone/tendon visible
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Both Stage 3 and Stage 4 are MCC — same DRG impact, but clinical accuracy matters.

specificity_ladder.description: Pressure ulcer of unspecified site, stage 4
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: MCC but unspecified site — query for anatomic location for accuracy.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Wound documented as 'pressure sore' or 'decubitus' without NPUAP staging
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented a pressure injury on the [LOCATION]. For coding accuracy, could you specify the NPUAP stage? Stage 3 = full-thickness skin loss (subQ visible). Stage 4 = full-thickness tissue loss (bone/tendon/muscle visible).

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Wound care notes show Stage 3 or 4 pressure ulcer but physician documentation is less specific
cdi_query_templates.query: Wound care nursing assessment documents a Stage [3/4] pressure ulcer of the [LOCATION]. Your documentation notes '[THEIR TERM].' Could you confirm the stage and location for coding alignment?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Pressure ulcer documented without laterality on a paired site
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented a pressure ulcer of the [hip/heel/buttock/elbow]. For accurate coding, is this the right or left side?

common_pitfalls: Only Stage 3 and Stage 4 pressure ulcers are CC/MCC (all are MCC). Stage 1, Stage 2, unstageable, and deep tissue are NOT CC/MCC.
'Unstageable' pressure ulcer is NOT the same as Stage 3 or 4 — unstageable means the wound bed is obscured by slough/eschar. It is not MCC.
Pressure ulcer present on admission (POA) still counts for CC/MCC and DRG assignment — POA only affects HAC processing.
Non-pressure chronic ulcers (L97.x) are different codes with different CC/MCC rules. Don't conflate with pressure ulcers (L89.x).
If a pressure ulcer progresses during the stay (e.g., Stage 2 at admission → Stage 3), code only the highest stage documented.

context_modifiers.with_osteomyelitis: Stage 4 pressure ulcer with exposed bone: query for osteomyelitis (M86.x). If present, code both — osteomyelitis adds additional DRG impact.
context_modifiers.with_sepsis: Infected pressure ulcer as source of sepsis: code the sepsis (A41.x), the pressure ulcer, and the infection. Document the causal chain.
context_modifiers.with_malnutrition: Malnutrition impairs wound healing and is commonly comorbid. Ensure both are documented and coded — combined CC/MCC impact is significant.

families.name: Encephalopathy
families.notes: G92 toxic, G93.4x metabolic/other, G93.5 compression, G93.6 edema, G93.7 Reye's, G93.82 brain death

clinical_criteria.criterion: Altered mental status: confusion, delirium, obtundation, or coma not explained by primary neurological diagnosis
clinical_criteria.detail: Must be distinguished from delirium (F05), dementia (F01-F03), and psychiatric conditions. The underlying cause determines the specific encephalopathy code.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Q2 2017 p.8: Encephalopathy documentation and coding

clinical_criteria.criterion: Metabolic derangements: hepatic (elevated ammonia, bilirubin), uremic (elevated BUN/Cr), hypoglycemic, electrolyte, or acid-base abnormalities
clinical_criteria.detail: Metabolic encephalopathy (G93.41, MCC) requires an identifiable metabolic cause. Labs should demonstrate the derangement causing altered mental status.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: AASLD Guidelines: Hepatic encephalopathy — ammonia criteria; General: metabolic workup

clinical_criteria.criterion: EEG showing diffuse slowing, triphasic waves, or periodic patterns consistent with encephalopathy
clinical_criteria.detail: EEG can support the diagnosis and help exclude seizure as the cause of altered mental status. Triphasic waves suggest metabolic (especially hepatic) encephalopathy.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: General: EEG findings supporting encephalopathy diagnosis

clinical_criteria.criterion: Identified toxic exposure: medications (opioids, sedatives, chemotherapy), substances, or environmental toxins
clinical_criteria.detail: Toxic encephalopathy (G92.8/G92.9) requires an identified toxic agent. Code the toxic agent/drug as additional code.
clinical_criteria.source_detail: Section I.C.6: Code the underlying toxic cause

documentation_requirements.requirement: Provider must document the TYPE of encephalopathy: metabolic (G93.41, MCC), toxic (G92.8, MCC), anoxic (G93.1, CC), or unspecified (G93.40, CC). The type determines MCC vs CC.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q2 2017 p.8: Encephalopathy vs AMS — provider must specify encephalopathy

documentation_requirements.requirement: Document the underlying CAUSE: hepatic, uremic, septic, hypoxic/anoxic, toxic (specify agent), or metabolic (specify derangement). This drives code selection and additional coding.
documentation_requirements.source_detail: Q3 2017 p.18: Specify the type and cause of encephalopathy

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Altered mental status with identifiable metabolic cause: hepatic, uremic, electrolyte, endocrine
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify 'metabolic' encephalopathy — changes CC to MCC

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Encephalopathy due to identified toxic agent: medication, substance, environmental
specificity_ladder.upgrade_evidence: Specify 'toxic' encephalopathy with causative agent

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Imaging showing cerebral edema (CT/MRI), elevated ICP, or clinical signs of herniation
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Distinct condition — may co-occur with encephalopathy but is separately codeable

specificity_ladder.distinguishing_evidence: Formal brain death determination per institutional protocol: absent brainstem reflexes, apnea test, confirmatory test
specificity_ladder.specificity_note: Terminal diagnosis — code when formally declared
CC tier only. Query for type — metabolic (G93.41) or toxic (G92.8) would be MCC.
CC tier — used for brain damage from hypoxic event. Different from metabolic encephalopathy.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Altered mental status documented but no encephalopathy diagnosis
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient has documented altered mental status with [lab findings — elevated ammonia, metabolic derangements, toxic exposure]. Does this clinical picture represent encephalopathy? If so, is this metabolic, toxic, or another type?

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Encephalopathy documented as 'unspecified' but clinical evidence suggests metabolic cause
cdi_query_templates.query: You documented 'encephalopathy.' Labs show [FINDINGS — ammonia elevated, hepatic dysfunction, uremia]. Would you characterize this as metabolic encephalopathy? Specifying the type allows for more accurate severity classification.

cdi_query_templates.trigger: Septic patient with AMS — encephalopathy not documented
cdi_query_templates.query: The patient has sepsis and developed altered mental status on [DATE] without an alternative neurological explanation. Does this represent septic encephalopathy (metabolic encephalopathy due to sepsis)?

common_pitfalls: 'Altered mental status' (R41.82) is a symptom, NOT a diagnosis. If encephalopathy is the cause, the provider must document 'encephalopathy.'
G93.40 (encephalopathy, unspecified) is only CC; G93.41 (metabolic encephalopathy) is MCC — specifying the type is the key CC→MCC upgrade.
'Delirium' (F05) is a separate diagnosis from encephalopathy. In many inpatient settings, both may apply — delirium is the clinical syndrome, encephalopathy is the neurological condition.
Hepatic encephalopathy should be coded with the underlying liver disease code. The encephalopathy code (G93.41) captures the neurological manifestation.
Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity (ICANS) grades 3-5 (G92.03–G92.05) are CC, not MCC — despite the severity, the CC/MCC assignment differs from other encephalopathies.

context_modifiers.with_liver_disease: Hepatic encephalopathy: code G93.41 (metabolic) + underlying liver disease (K72.x, K70.x). Ammonia level supports but is not required for the diagnosis.
context_modifiers.with_sepsis: Septic encephalopathy: code G93.41 (metabolic) + sepsis codes. AMS in sepsis without other neurological cause is likely septic encephalopathy.
context_modifiers.with_ckd: Uremic encephalopathy: code G93.41 + N18.x (CKD stage). Typically occurs with very high BUN/creatinine. Dialysis initiation may resolve it.

families.name: COPD / Asthma Exacerbation
families.notes: J43 emphysema, J44 COPD, J45 asthma

families.name: Pulmonary Embolism
families.notes: I26.x: With/without acute cor pulmonale

families.name: Atrial Fibrillation / Flutter
families.notes: I48.x: Paroxysmal, persistent, chronic, typical/atypical flutter

families.name: GI Hemorrhage
families.notes: K92.0–K92.2 + K25–K29 with hemorrhage

families.name: Acute Pancreatitis
families.notes: K85.x: By etiology (biliary, alcohol, drug, idiopathic)

families.name: Hepatic Failure / Encephalopathy
families.notes: K70 alcoholic liver, K71 toxic liver, K72 hepatic failure

families.name: DIC / Coagulopathy
families.notes: D65 DIC, D68 other coag defects, D69 purpura/hemorrhagic

families.name: Acute Blood Loss Anemia
families.notes: D62: Single code, CC — commonly queried

families.name: Diabetes with Complications
families.notes: E08–E13: By type and complication (neuropathy, CKD, PVD, etc.)

families.name: Electrolyte Disorders
families.notes: E87.x: Hypo/hypernatremia, acidosis, alkalosis, volume depletion

families.name: Sickle Cell Disease
families.notes: D57.x: Hb-SS, Hb-SC, thalassemia with/without crisis

families.name: Cardiac Arrest
families.notes: I46.x: VF, VT, asystole — only assigned if patient survives

families.name: Shock
families.notes: R57.x: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, other/unspecified

families.name: ARDS
families.notes: J80: Single code, MCC — Berlin Definition criteria

families.name: Intestinal Ischemia
families.notes: K55.x: Acute/chronic vascular disorders of intestine

families.name: Traumatic Brain Injury
families.notes: S06.x: Concussion, contusion, hemorrhage by LOC duration

families.name: Spinal Cord Injury
families.notes: S14/S24/S34: Cervical/thoracic/lumbar by level and completeness

families.name: Vascular Injuries
families.notes: Arterial/venous injury by body region

families.name: Traumatic Fractures
families.notes: Skull/spine/rib/pelvis/upper/lower extremity fractures

families.name: Pathological Fractures
families.notes: M80 osteoporotic, M84 stress/other pathological

families.name: Burns / Corrosions
families.notes: T20–T32: By site, depth, TBSA

families.name: Meningitis / Encephalitis
families.notes: Bacterial/viral/other meningitis and encephalitis

families.name: Specific Infections (C.diff, MRSA, etc.)
families.notes: Organism identification and resistance codes

families.name: Neoplasms
families.notes: All malignant + in-situ + benign-uncertain neoplasms with CC/MCC

families.name: Obstetric Complications
families.notes: O00–O9A: By trimester and complication type

families.name: Perinatal Conditions
families.notes: P00–P96: Birth weight, gestational age, neonatal conditions

families.name: Congenital Anomalies
families.notes: Q00–Q99: By system

families.name: Substance Use Disorders
families.notes: F10–F19: By substance, use/abuse/dependence, with complications

families.name: Epilepsy / Status Epilepticus
families.notes: G40.x status epilepticus, intractability; G41 not used in ICD-10-CM FY2026 but reserved

families.name: Eye Conditions
families.notes: H00–H59: Inflammatory, degenerative, vascular eye conditions

families.name: Ear Conditions
families.notes: H60–H95: Ear and mastoid conditions

families.name: Aortic Conditions
families.notes: I71.x: Dissection, aneurysm by location, ruptured vs unruptured

families.name: Cerebrovascular Hemorrhage
families.notes: I60 SAH, I61 ICH, I62 other nontraumatic intracranial hemorrhage

families.name: Postprocedural / Device Complications
families.notes: T80–T88: Transfusion, procedural, device, graft complications

families.name: CKD / Renal Conditions
families.notes: N18 CKD staging, N00–N08 glomerular, N10–N13 tubulointerstitial

families.name: Other Respiratory Conditions
families.notes: Remaining J chapter: Bronchiolitis, pleural effusion, lung abscess, pneumothorax, tracheostomy complications, postprocedural respiratory

families.name: Other Infectious Diseases
families.notes: Remaining A/B chapter codes: TB, syphilis, viral, parasitic, fungal

families.name: Other Cardiovascular Conditions
families.notes: Remaining I chapter: DVT/VTE, PVD, cardiomyopathy, endocarditis, valvular, etc.

families.name: Other Digestive Conditions
families.notes: Remaining K chapter: Liver disease, bowel obstruction, peritonitis, etc.

families.name: Other Skin Conditions
families.notes: Remaining L chapter: Non-pressure ulcers, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis

families.name: Other Musculoskeletal Conditions
families.notes: Remaining M chapter: Osteomyelitis, avascular necrosis, other bone/joint

families.name: Other Nervous System Conditions
families.notes: Remaining G chapter: Paralysis, hydrocephalus, neuromuscular, demyelinating

families.name: Other Mental Health Conditions
families.notes: Remaining F chapter: Dementia, schizophrenia, bipolar, eating disorders

families.name: Other Endocrine / Metabolic
families.notes: Remaining E chapter: Other metabolic, nutritional, endocrine

families.name: Other Genitourinary Conditions
families.notes: Remaining N chapter: UTI complications, obstruction, other renal

families.name: Other Blood / Immune Conditions
families.notes: Remaining D chapter: Anemias, immune disorders, not DIC/sickle cell

families.name: Other Traumatic Injuries
families.notes: Remaining S chapter: Dislocations, organ injuries, amputations, crushing

families.name: Other Injuries / Poisoning / External
families.notes: Remaining T chapter: Frostbite, asphyxiation, abuse, anaphylaxis, poisoning

families.name: Signs / Symptoms / Coma Scales
families.notes: Remaining R chapter: Coma scales, SIRS, respiratory arrest, cachexia

families.name: Health Status / Transplant / Resistance
families.notes: Z codes with CC/MCC: Transplant status, resistance codes, etc.

families.name: COVID-19
families.notes: U07.1: COVID-19
